nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry cr_id F06090031 (confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients (vitamins B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, C, A, D, E, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 10062 — Pork, fresh, loin, center rib (chops), bone-in, separable lean and fat, raw — as these fields are not reported in the HPB entry. Both HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.organization: N/A — University of Sydney GI Database not applicable for zero-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pork midloin chop, raw contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06090031). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates and produces no glycaemic response. Per Project Veritas v2 critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods: glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to zero-carbohydrate animal protein foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (55 mg/100 g) with high potassium (343 mg/100 g) yields a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Raw pork midloin chop is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt, high-sodium marinades, or sauces; grilling or baking without seasoning preserves the naturally low sodium content and supports blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content means this food has no glycaemic impact; high protein (20.71 g/100 g) supports satiety and attenuates postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork midloin chop is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a lean protein source with no carbohydrate load; portion control of total fat is advised, and the chop should be paired with high-fibre vegetables and whole grains to achieve a balanced, low-GI meal pattern.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is 9.03 g/100 g with saturated fat at approximately 3.13 g/100 g; moderate saturated fat intake requires attention in the context of LDL-cholesterol management for cardiovascular disease.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may include pork midloin chop in moderation (up to 2–3 servings per week) provided visible fat is trimmed before cooking, preparation avoids added saturated fats, and the overall dietary pattern aligns with heart-healthy guidelines emphasising unsaturated fats, fibre, and reduced sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which can promote pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to rheumatoid arthritis disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat including pork to 1–2 servings per week per EULAR anti-inflammatory dietary guidelines, trim visible fat before cooking to reduce arachidonic acid load, and balance intake with anti-inflammatory foods such as fatty fish, leafy greens, and omega-3-rich sources.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork midloin chop, raw, is a bone-in cut of pork taken from the loin section of the pig, situated between the rib and sirloin areas. In its raw state it displays a pale pink to light red colour with firm, dense muscle texture, surrounded by a thin layer of external fat and containing internal intramuscular fat marbling. The cut includes the vertebral bone, which contributes to its characteristic T-bone or rib-chop appearance depending on the exact position. Per 100 g, it provides 170 kcal, 20.71 g protein, 9.03 g total fat (approximately 3.13 g saturated), and 0 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is low at 55 mg/100 g and potassium is high at 343 mg/100 g, supporting a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio aligned with DASH dietary principles for blood pressure management. Cholesterol is 69 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Clinically, this cut is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension when prepared without added salt or sugary marinades; cardiovascular disease patients should trim visible fat and limit frequency. Pork loin is a versatile raw ingredient used globally in grilling, pan-frying, braising, and roasting applications. It is a staple protein in Western, East Asian, and Southeast Asian cuisines.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06090031, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2. Fatty acid breakdown and additional micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 10062) for pork loin chop, raw, which is an approved secondary source. Both sources are explicitly listed in the approved source policy. The food identity is unambiguous and the source match is exact for the primary source.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as pork is a pure animal protein and fat food with no carbohydrate content; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06090031). Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle meat product containing no plant-derived fibre; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 10062) for pork loin, raw; naturally occurring ruminant trans fats are not present in pork at measurable levels. Saturated_fat_g (3.13 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.99 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.87 g), and micronutrient values (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 10062, Pork, fresh, loin, center rib (chops), bone-in, separable lean and fat, raw) as these fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per Project Veritas v2 critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under the multilingual naming rules (G6). Halal status is not confirmed as pork is not halal; is_halal set to false.
